evidence_quotes.quote: Game developers and publishers stop operating online services or whole games, even if they are content for which users have paid for.
The permanent loss of a paid digital product without a substitute should be classified as an unfair practice.
transparent information before a game or service is switched off
Paid digital products should not be allowed to be switched off.
It should be clarified that digital goods for which a fee has been paid must be below a minimum useful life and accessibility.

paragraphs.text: Subject: I would urge you to include safeguards against switching off paid games in the Digital Services Act, I would like to urge you to include specific rules under the Digital Services Act (DSA) to protect consumers from losing access to digital games and services they have legally acquired. In recent years, a worrying practice has emerged: Game developers and publishers stop operating online services or whole games, even if they are content for which users have paid for. In many cases, this permanently deprives consumers of access to their digital purchases without receiving compensation or alternative usage. This call is also supported by the Stop Killing Games Movement, which receives great attention across Europe. The related EU citizens’ initiative has already collected more than 1.4 million signatures, a clear signal that this issue is at the heart of many people in the EU and that it should be addressed as a matter of urgency. This practice is highly problematic from the point of view of consumer protection law. Paid digital products should not be allowed to be switched off. I therefore propose that the DSA be strengthened in this area, in particular by: Providers of digital content should be required to provide transparent information before a game or service is switched off and to offer a fair end-of-life regime (e.g. offline mode or server emulation). The permanent loss of a paid digital product without a substitute should be classified as an unfair practice. It should be clarified that digital goods for which a fee has been paid must be below a minimum useful life and accessibility. The Digital Services Act provides a unique opportunity to strengthen digital consumer rights in Europe and limit the abuse of dominant digital gaming structures. I call on you to put the protection of digital tenure rights at the heart of future regulation. Yours sincerely,
